title: Cybersecurity: Malware
subtitle: Know Your Enemy — Viruses, Trojans, Ransomware & More!
overview: Every day, thousands of cyberattacks happen around the world — targeting schools, hospitals, banks, and individuals. In this session, students learn about the different types of malware: what they are, how they spread, what damage they cause, and most importantly how to defend against them. From classic computer viruses to modern ransomware, students become cyber-aware citizens who can protect themselves and others in the digital world.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — malware type diagrams, ransomware screenshots, infection flowcharts, defence checklist.

keyTopics: Malware — Malicious Software: any program designed to disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system
Virus — Malware that attaches itself to a legitimate file and replicates when that file is executed, spreading to other files and systems
Worm — Self-replicating malware that spreads across networks automatically WITHOUT needing a host file or user action
Trojan Horse — Malware disguised as a legitimate and useful program; tricks the user into installing it willingly
Ransomware — Encrypts the victim's files and demands a ransom payment (usually cryptocurrency) to restore access
Spyware — Secretly monitors user activity, captures keystrokes, passwords, and browsing history, and sends data to attackers
Adware — Displays unwanted advertisements, often bundled with free software; can slow down devices and redirect browsers
Rootkit — Malware that hides deep in the operating system, granting attackers persistent admin-level access while remaining invisible
Botnet — A network of infected computers (bots) controlled remotely by an attacker to launch coordinated attacks like DDoS
Attack Vector — The method used to deliver malware: email attachments, malicious downloads, infected USB drives, phishing links
Antivirus / Anti-malware — Software that detects, quarantines, and removes known malware using signature databases and behavioural analysis
Defence in Depth — A layered security strategy: update software + use antivirus + use firewall + backup data + educate users

activity.title: Malware Identification Card Game + Defence Strategy Planning
parts: Printed Malware Scenario Cards (one per group of 3–4 students)
Malware Type Reference Sheet (virus, worm, trojan, ransomware, spyware, adware, rootkit)
Computer or tablet (for research and verification)
Whiteboard or flip chart for class discussion
steps: Part 1 — Malware Identification Game: Each group receives 6 scenario cards describing a cyber incident. Students identify the malware type, the attack vector used, and the damage caused. Groups compete to correctly identify all 6 first

Part 2 — Real Attack Case Study: Discuss the WannaCry ransomware attack (2017) — how it spread through a Windows vulnerability, why the NHS was affected, and what the global cost was. Students answer: what could have prevented it?

Part 3 — Attack Vector Mapping: Students list 5 ways malware can enter a computer (email attachment, USB drive, malicious website, fake app, infected ad). For each, identify one thing a user can do to block that vector

Part 4 — Defence Strategy: Each group designs a 'Cybersecurity Policy' for a fictional school computer lab — at least 6 rules covering software updates, antivirus, backups, USB policy, download policy, and user training
Part 5 — Trojan Detection Challenge: Students examine 5 fake 'app descriptions' and identify which ones are likely Trojan horses based on suspicious permissions, promises, or sources
Part 6 — Class Debate: 'Should victims pay ransomware demands?' Students argue for and against, considering ethics, financial pressure, and whether paying encourages more attacks

Challenge: Students research one real-world malware attack (e.g. Stuxnet, Petya, ILOVEYOU, Zeus, Mirai) and present: What type of malware? Who was targeted? How did it spread? What was the impact? How was it stopped?

quiz.q: What does the term Malware mean?
options: Malfunctioning hardware
Malicious Software — any program designed to disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorised access to a system
A type of computer virus found only on Macs
Software that runs slowly due to a bug
quiz.explanation: Malware = Malicious Software! It's an umbrella term covering all types of harmful programs — viruses, worms, trojans, ransomware, spyware, and more. The intent to cause harm is what defines something as malware.

quiz.q: What makes a Computer Virus different from other malware?
options: A virus can only infect smartphones, not computers
A virus attaches itself to a legitimate file and replicates when that file is executed, spreading to other files
A virus always demands a ransom payment
A virus only affects antivirus software
quiz.explanation: Like a biological virus, a computer virus needs a HOST — it attaches to a legitimate file. When that file runs, the virus executes and copies itself into other files. It needs user action (running the infected file) to spread.

quiz.q: How is a Worm different from a Virus?
options: Worms are more dangerous than viruses
A worm spreads across networks automatically without needing a host file or any user action
Worms only target mobile phones
A worm always encrypts files like ransomware
quiz.explanation: Worms are self-sufficient! Unlike viruses, they don't need a host file. They exploit network vulnerabilities to copy themselves from device to device automatically — no user clicks required. WannaCry used a worm mechanism to spread across networks at terrifying speed.

quiz.q: Why is it called a Trojan Horse in cybersecurity?
options: It was invented in Troy, Greece
Like the Greek myth, it disguises itself as something useful or safe to trick the user into letting it inside their system
It attacks using a horse-shaped robot
It was the first malware ever created in the 1960s
quiz.explanation: Perfect analogy from Greek mythology! The Greeks hid soldiers inside a wooden horse gifted to Troy. Similarly, a Trojan Horse program hides malicious code inside something that looks legitimate — a free game, a useful app, or a fake update.

quiz.q: What does Ransomware do to a victim's computer?
options: Steals passwords and sends them to the attacker
Displays unwanted advertisements on the screen
Encrypts the victim's files and demands payment — usually cryptocurrency — to restore access
Slows down the computer by running in the background
quiz.explanation: Ransomware holds your data hostage! It encrypts files so you can't open them, then displays a ransom note demanding payment (often Bitcoin) for the decryption key. Hospitals, schools, and businesses have paid millions — sometimes still not recovering their data.

quiz.q: What is Spyware designed to do?
options: Display pop-up advertisements
Encrypt files and demand payment
Secretly monitor user activity, capturing keystrokes, passwords, and browsing history without the user's knowledge
Spread copies of itself to other computers on the network
quiz.explanation: Spyware spies on you silently! Keyloggers (a type of spyware) record every key you press — capturing passwords, credit card numbers, and private messages. The data is quietly sent to attackers. You may not know it's there for months.

options: A network of antivirus programs working together
A collection of internet robots used for customer service
A network of infected computers controlled remotely by an attacker, used to launch coordinated attacks
A type of firewall that blocks bots
quiz.explanation: A Botnet is an army of zombie computers! Each infected device (a 'bot') is controlled remotely by an attacker (the 'bot herder'). Botnets are used to send millions of spam emails, launch DDoS attacks, or mine cryptocurrency — all using the victims' computing power.

quiz.q: What is a DDoS attack, often carried out using a Botnet?
options: Data Deletion of Systems — erasing files from a server
Distributed Denial of Service — flooding a server with traffic from thousands of infected machines until it crashes and becomes unavailable
Direct Download of Software — stealing data from a company server
Digital Defence of Systems — a cybersecurity protection technique
quiz.explanation: DDoS = Distributed Denial of Service! Attackers command thousands of botnet machines to simultaneously flood a target website or server with requests. The server gets overwhelmed and crashes — making the service unavailable to real users. Banks and gaming platforms are common targets.

quiz.q: Which of these is an Attack Vector — a way malware enters a device?
options: Installing an official app from the Google Play Store
Opening an email attachment from an unknown sender that contains a disguised executable file
Connecting to a trusted WiFi network at home
Shutting down the computer properly
quiz.explanation: Email attachments are one of the most common attack vectors! A malicious file disguised as a PDF, invoice, or document can execute malware when opened. Other vectors include infected USB drives, malicious websites, fake software updates, and phishing links.

options: A tool used by developers to access the root folder of a project
Malware that hides deep in the operating system, granting attackers persistent admin-level access while remaining invisible to the user
A kit for repairing corrupted system files
A type of antivirus that scans the root directory
quiz.explanation: Rootkits are the masters of stealth! They embed themselves at the OS level, hiding their presence from antivirus tools and even the task manager. Attackers use rootkits to maintain long-term, hidden access to a system — sometimes going undetected for years.

quiz.q: How does Antivirus software detect known malware?
options: It physically removes the hard drive and scans it with a laser
It uses a signature database — comparing files on the device against known patterns of malware code
It connects to the internet and asks a human expert
It only detects malware if the user reports it manually
quiz.explanation: Signature detection is like a fingerprint database! Every known malware has a unique code pattern (signature). Antivirus software scans files and compares them to millions of known signatures. If a match is found — quarantine! Regular updates keep the database current for new threats.

quiz.q: Why should you NEVER plug in a found USB drive of unknown origin?
options: USB drives from unknown sources may not be compatible with your computer
Attackers deliberately leave infected USB drives in public places — plugging one in can automatically execute malware and infect your computer
USB drives slow down computers permanently
Plugging in a USB drive voids your computer warranty
quiz.explanation: The 'lost USB' trick is a real social engineering attack! Attackers leave infected USB drives in car parks, offices, and school halls knowing curious people will plug them in. Auto-run features can execute malware the moment the drive is connected. Never plug in unknown USB drives!

quiz.q: What is the MOST important reason to keep your operating system and software updated?
options: Updates add new emojis and themes
Updates patch security vulnerabilities that malware like worms and trojans actively exploit to infect systems
Updates make your computer run faster permanently
Updates are only necessary for gaming software
quiz.explanation: Security patches are critical! WannaCry ransomware exploited a Windows vulnerability that Microsoft had already patched — but millions of users hadn't updated. Attackers constantly hunt for unpatched systems. Keeping software updated closes the doors they use to get in.

quiz.q: What is the 3-2-1 Backup Rule — an essential defence against ransomware?
options: Back up 3 times a day, 2 folders, 1 drive
Keep 3 copies of your data, on 2 different types of storage media, with 1 copy stored offsite or in the cloud
Use 3 passwords, 2 antiviruses, and 1 firewall
Scan your system 3 times, delete 2 threats, and report 1 to authorities
quiz.explanation: The 3-2-1 rule is the gold standard backup strategy! 3 copies of data, 2 on different media (e.g. hard drive + USB), 1 offsite or in the cloud. If ransomware encrypts your files, you restore from the clean backup — no need to pay the ransom!

quiz.q: Should a ransomware victim always pay the ransom demanded by attackers?
options: Yes — paying is the fastest way to recover files
No — paying does not guarantee file recovery, funds criminal activity, and encourages more attacks on others
Yes — law enforcement always recommends paying
Only pay if the ransom is under ₹10,000
quiz.explanation: Most cybersecurity experts and law enforcement advise against paying! There is no guarantee attackers will provide the decryption key. Paying funds criminal organisations and signals that attacks are profitable — encouraging more. The best defence is prevention and backups.
